text: We present the case of a fourteen-year-old female with a one-year history of diffuse abdominal pain causing vomiting that worsened two weeks prior to being seen in our emergency department. Her medical history was significant for depression. On physical examination, a mass was palpable from the pigastrium to the umbilical area.
CT scan revealed a mass that was contained in the stomach. This mass was characteristic of a trichobesaur.

During laparoscopy, the surgeon identifies a rigid stomach due to the presence of a mass within the gastric lumen. The lesser sac is opened to mobilize the stomach for further laparoscopic surgical intervention.
The surgical team measures the size of the stomach to tailor a containment bag, aiming to prevent contamination of the peritoneal cavity during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform a gastrostomy, opening the stomach and revealing a thin gastric wall. The contents of the stomach, including components of the trichobezoar, are visible through the approximately ten-centimeter incision.
The surgeon carefully peels off the stomach wall to expose the bezoar, attempting to pull it into a specimen bag while minimizing injury to the gastric tissue.
The surgeon uses gentle traction to reduce and pull the tail of the bezoar, associated with Rapunzel syndrome, from the small bowel back into the gastric cavity.
It is very important to be able to reduce this tail in its totality. There are cases reported where this besoar tail can travel down to the ileocecal valve.

The surgeon places the bezoar specimen completely inside a retrieval bag, closes the bag, and extracts it through a funnestal incision, which is noted for minimizing pain and improving cosmetic outcomes.
The surgeon uses round clamps to remove the specimen from the patient's abdomen. The funnestal incision is visible, having been closed and washed, followed by the closure of the gastrotomy.
The surgeon closes the incision using a monofilament absorbable suture in a running fashion, completing the closure in a single layer.
The stomach will be next tested for Ehrlich
The operative time was eighty minutes. The patient had an uneventful post-operative course, was able to be discharged in post-op day four.

Trichobesolar is an atypical cause of gastric outlet obstruction. It needs to be suspected in patients with psychiatric disorders. A large trichobesolar can be managed laparoscopically with minimal contamination. A financial incision is always a good option to remove a large specimen with good cosmetic results.
